item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is excellent, I will say the above comments that call out the lack of mentioning the vagina is pretty glaring… I think you could get around this by saying a simple line of “in addition…”

That being said, it wasn’t enough to kill what is a haunting implication by the ending.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. It's a pretty glaring error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the chest one, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is so much this tiny little article manages to evoke. No resolute conclusion, no easy answers out, and above all, an uncomfortably delightful aftertaste. +1, no doubt about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I interpreted it as the aftereffects of a powerful antimeme: whatever implanted and removed these objects made it the holes extremely difficult to notice, but it's possible to brute-force your way into noticing them if A) the subject is dead, and B) you're explicitly trying to find them. Because 'everyone knows' that people don't have tiny holes in their foreheads, no-one ever paid enough attention to override the effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A series of words I never thought I'd have to write:

This is excellent, in fact it's one of the best ultra-short-form articles I've read, but I'm going to have to downvote until you fix the glaring omission of any reference to the vaginal opening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the implication is that the anomaly is so unknown precisely _because_ there's no debate over it. It's not triggered just by performing an autopsy, but by performing an autopsy with the express purpose of disproving the assumption on the number of orifices, and thus only the weirdest of studies is going to come across it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The widespread nature of this SCP's effect makes it totally implausible to me, I would have upvoted for a neat weird thing if it were only on X subsets or some other criteria made it not universal among the human species, it doesn't seem like it would be possible for this to be suppressed from public knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As well as the ocular orbits, milk ducts, sweat pores, and hair follicles.

I like what the article is going for, but the line "The majority of anatomical scientists have concluded that humans possess seven openings" just feels weird. Why are "anatomical scientists" having debates about the number of openings people have? The anomaly is supposedly a secret known only to the foundation and one retired surgeon.

The whole story would be better without the list of body parts. Especially without the "male-as-default" implication tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, but approximately 51% do, so you can't write it off. It should be mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, it says "humans", and I'm not sure that every human has one of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is going in my no-context-screenshots folder, thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …You forgot the vagina.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is lost? The third eye? The soul? Something different?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this but I"m somewhat worried that we're missing an opening here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so scary that I almost cried. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The saying that "less is more" was wonderfully applied here, you managed to make extremely subtle and disturbing article in a mere seven (short!) paragraphs without bombarding the reader with tons of addenda, interview logs, etc. Excellent work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Expertly constructed to be very subtly worrying. Well done :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels incomplete. I like the anomaly, but it feels like this piece is missing something. Simple is good, but this is too simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderfully weird

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It doesn't bog me the reader down with exposition and explanation. It wastes no time in telling me the abnormality (examined cadavers universally display the fact that something was removed from the body), and frames it in a very professional/journalistic format. I go in, get creeped out, and leave feeling chilled. That's all that was needed to sell me on this one.

Please take your upvote, +1
